data.title: Proximus PLC 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Proximus PLC in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: 68 Graham All-Star Value Dogs (GASV): Of 18 "Safer", Buy Any Of 14 Ideal February Choices
data.description:
Foreword

Definition excerpts below from YCharts:

About Large Cap Value

“A Value ranking for large cap stocks from YCharts puts together complementary strategies found during their stock research. The value ranking looks at the price of a stock relative to a number of measurements that determine intrinsic firm value. Only the largest 10% of companies based on market cap are shown in this portfolio.

What it is: The Value Score is a composite score. It tells you how much you are getting in terms of profits, cash flows, assets, sales, etc. for the stock price that you pay. It is a relative measurement, so it says nothing about the overall level of the market. Rather, it answers the question: "Given the current market level, which stocks give you the most current value for your dollar of investment?" Tens are the most value, and 1s are the least.

How to use it: Use it to find companies that are selling at a low price relative to their assets and profits.

Limitations of the Value Score: Watch out for companies with a lot of uncertainty or bad prospects for the future (e.g., Pharma companies with expiring patents, industries on the decline, etc.).

About the Ben Graham Formula

The Ben Graham Formula strategy contains ultra-stable stocks that will infrequently lose money if held over a long period of time. It was developed based on a screen in Graham's book, "The Intelligent Investor." For those who have the book, it is the "Defensive Investor" screen. It selects stocks that are large in terms of sales and total assets, have a strong track record of earnings and dividend payments, have a reasonable current ratio and level of long term debt, and have a low valuation given by PE Ratios and Price to Book Value ratios.”

-YCharts

While over half of this collection of GASV is too pricey, or reveal somewhat skinny or no forward looking dividends, fourteen of the eighteen “safer” lowest-priced Dogs of the GASV are ready to buy. February finds HSBC Holdings (HSBC), KS KKR Capital (FSK), Altria Group (MO), EnergyTransfer (ET), DHT Holdings (DHT),Verizon (VZ), First Interstate (FIBK), Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS), Columbia Banking (COLB), Suncor Energy (SU), Barclays (BCS), Banco Santander (SAN), Kronos Worldwide (KRO), on the buy list.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced February GASV Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and all eighteen safe buys at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 39.75% To 82.26% Net Gains From Top-Ten GASV Dogs By February 2026

Five of ten top GASV (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this February, 2025 yield-based forecast for them, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to February, 2026 were:

Pangaea Logistics (PANL) was projected to net $842.55 based on the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 66% under the market as a whole.

International Seaways (INSW) was projected to net $803.91, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 8 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 87% under the market as a whole.

Guess? (GES) was projected to net $682.31, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 89% greater than the market as a whole.

Dorian LPG (LPG) was projected to net $633.06 based on the median of target prices estimated by 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 2% under the market as a whole.

TORM PLC (TRMD) was projected to net $593.17 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 77% less than the market as a whole.

Petrobras (PBR) was projected to net $521.61, based on the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 41% greater than the market as a whole.

Jiayin Group (JFIN) was projected to net $440.89, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this 11% under the market as a whole.

Vale SA (VALE) was projected to net $436.22, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% under the market as a whole.

Park Hotels (PK) was projected to net $423.20, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 14 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 100% over the market as a whole.

The RMR Group (RMR) was projected to net $397.53 based on the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 24% greater than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 57.75% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten GASV stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 24% greater than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

Top 50 February, 2025 GASV By Analyst Target Gains

Top 50 February, 2025 GASV By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top February GASV Stocks By Yield Ranged 11.96% To 38.9% Per YCharts

Top ten GASV occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Five energy representatives placed first, second, and fifth, to seventh, Petrobas, [1], TORM [2], International Seaways [5], Dorian [6], and Flex LNG (FLNG) [7].

One materials stock placed third, Vale [3] and a lone real estate member placed fourth, Site Centers (SITC) [4]. A discretionary sector dog took the eighth position, Ford Motor (F) [8].

Finally, two financials stalwarts, placed ninth, and tenth, HSBC [9], and FS KKR [10], to complete the February Dogs of the GASV by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten February GASV by dividend and price as of market close 2/25/2025.

This month none of the top-ten GASV by yield show less than ideal status because the prices of all ten are less than the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each. Yes, all ten are IDEAL.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by forty-nine of the highest yield GASV. The 68 January GASV Cost Per $1 of dividend ranged from $2.57 for Petrobras, to $155.55 for Itau Unibanco.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Twenty-one of Sixty-eight GASV Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Twenty-one GASV so infected are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, and the toll of trading tariffs.

No Price Drops or Dividend Increases Are Needed; All Ten Top Yield GASV Are ”Fair-Priced” For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, all the top ten are already ideally priced. The results from the bottom chart show that at the tipping point between fair and unfair, the top ten GASV have margins ranging from $10.44 to $60.11 or 16.1% to 85.72% before they break into the unfair territory.

These companies have no need to raise their dividends. Mr. Market is very effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts. Watch and buy after your targeted stock price moves into a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 23.61% To 72.54% Top Ten GASV Upsides To February, 2025; (32) Three -8.16% to -15.21% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 4.57% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 GASV As of February 25, 2026

Ten-top GASVs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten GASV selected 2/25/25 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. Communication Services, Consumer Defensive, Health Care, Industrials, Technology, and Utilities, all missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend GASV (34) To Deliver 42.25% Vs. (35) 40.41% Net Gains by All Ten Come February 20, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten GASV by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 4.57% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The eighth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, INSW, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 80.29%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield GASV dogs for February were:

Ford; Vale; Petrobras; Site Centers; TORM, with prices ranging from $9.42 to $18.90.

Five higher-priced GASV top-yield dogs as of February 25 were: Dorian LPG; Flex LNG; FS KKR; International Seaways; HSBC, with prices ranging from $21.18 to $57.77.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria in the market dies down and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential tariff pronouncements gain momentum into 2025, with the exception of the few tech stocks, look for more GASV to become Fair-priced… slow and sure, or suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 14 Ideal of 18 ‘Safer' February GASV Dogs

Late-February 2025, 18 of the 68 GASV were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 2/25/25 data showed 14 were fair-priced.

How GASV 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Produced “Break-Even Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that ten dividends from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Aristocrats conform to the dogcatcher ideal of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those ten ideal dogs were HSBC, FS KKR, Altria; Energy Transfer, DHT, Verizon; First Interstate, Bank of Nova Scotia, Columbia Banking, and Trust Financial.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “break-even price.”

All ten share prices of these “safer” survivors are below or the dividend payouts from $1k invested. The top chart reveals that the ten show ranges of $4.63 to $29.50 or 0.99% to 56.78% in price increases to equal the dividend payout from $1k invested.

These GASV leaders fetch dividends about as “good” as they can get.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher GASV updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" and “Ideal” GASV dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; Yahoo Finance - Stock Market Live, Quotes, Business & Finance News; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from Dividend Dog Catcher

data.title: Repeated Disappointments On Growth And Margins Leaves Conmed Even Deeper In The Doghouse
data.description:
It is arguably unfair to say that nothing is going right for CONMED Corporation (NYSE:CNMD) (“Conmed”) these days, but there’s certainly not enough going right to get the stock working right now. Management’s prior revenue growth targets have proven much too ambitious and operational issues that I would argue should have been nixed some time ago continue to weigh on margins, and that’s without the potentially severe impact of tariffs on the business.

Conmed shares are down another 10% or so since my last update, underperforming the broader medical device space by close to 25%. While these shares do still look meaningfully undervalued, Conmed is looking at 2025 where a lot of effort will be expended without necessarily showing up in the final results. What’s more, the Street remains concerned with operational challenges to key growth products like AirSeal (from Intuitive Surgical, Inc.'s (ISRG) ongoing launch of its newest robot platform), and the reality is that mid-single-digit revenue growth and no margin leverage is a toxic mix for a small-cap med-tech stock.

I still think there’s value here, and patient investors may want to wait it out, but I can’t really say this is a great idea for new investment now.

A Good Quarter (Sort Of), But It Doesn’t Matter All That Much

I’ll discuss Conmed’s disappointing guidance (and potential downside risk to that guidance) in a moment, but a review of the fourth quarter is in order. While reported results were better than the Street expected, particularly at the operating income line, that should be viewed in the context that estimates came down throughout 2024 and the final results were weaker than what analysts had been expecting at the time of my last article.

Revenue rose 6% in organic terms, with Orthopedic Surgery revenue up more than 2% (and up more than 5% in the U.S.) and General Surgery up almost 9% (and up more than 7% in the U.S.). By product type, revenue from single-use products rose almost 10% (to almost 86% of the total), while capital equipment sales dropped more than 12%.

Adjusted gross margin rose 120bp (and 110bp sequentially) to 57.6%. Adjusted operating income rose 25%, with the margin up almost three points to 18.6%

Ortho sales continued to be limited by ongoing supply issues, and while AirSeal sales were up double-digits for the full year, Intuitive Surgical placements are going to pick up next year, creating some more near-term challenges (though Conmed has seen a lot of surgeons return to the product once they fulfill their procedural commitments).

Guidance Was The Falling Star Of The Show

Conmed management was clearly too aggressive with its expectations for 2024, and the backpedaling through the year certainly didn’t help sentiment, even if unexpected events like a hurricane impacting a Florida manufacturing facility are more or less forgivable. More to the point, the fact that supply issues continue to be an issue doesn’t reflect particularly well on the company.

With third quarter earnings the company’s now-former CEO announced his retirement and the company announced that the COO, Patrick Beyer, will take over, Since then guidance has come down further, and this could arguably be seen as the incoming CEO’s attempt to “clear the decks”, get all the bad news out there, and start more or less from a fresh page.

In any case, guidance for 2025 revenue was about 3% below already-lowered expectations and 4% to 6% revenue growth is well below the high single-digit to low double-digit growth that analysts were expecting from the business a year ago.

On top of that, management announced that they expected no margin expansion in 2025, as the company will be reinvesting in its supply efforts and working down inventories. On the supply side, the company ultimately realized it was too dependent upon single-source suppliers and now needs to remedy that issue. On the inventory side, the company simply has too much inventory built up in certain SKUs and is going to burn off that inventory, which will hurt manufacturing leverage in the near term.

That guidance was rough enough as it was (with a new EPS target range of $4.25 to $4.40 versus the Street’s prior $4.74), but it could get worse. Not only might further expansion of the Intuitive Surgical Da Vinci 5 rollout have a bigger-than-expected impact on AirSeal (one of Conmed’s best growth products), but tariffs could take a big bite out of earnings.

About a third of the company’s manufacturing takes place in a maquiladora in Mexico. If the threatened 25% tariff goes into effect, and there are no exemptions carved out for maquiladoras or consideration of value-added in Mexico, as much as 25% of company EPS could be at risk. Likewise, the expiration of an exemption to tariffs on products from China would add a small incremental negative effect on top of that.

There are still plenty of unknowns here. If the new tariff were to only apply to the value-add, it would reduce the impact by more than 80%, but I think that’s likely an optimistic assumption. It is also uncertain as to what the company may be able to do to minimize exposure – products manufactured in Mexico are shipped to a facility in Georgia for sterilization, and my understanding is that products meant for international markets could be shipped elsewhere, reducing the tariff impact.

The Outlook

It’s not all doom and gloom here. The company still has an overall attractive mix of businesses, including good exposure to above-average growth markets like foot/ankle, sports medicine, and smoke evacuation (where state-level mandates continue to expand the market). Moreover, AirSeal is far from dead, as surgeons who like the device continue to find ways to use it, and BioBrace bio-inductive implant could become a multi-hundred million dollar a year product for the company.

In the short term, though, there are obviously significant challenges that management must resolve. I’ve cut my 2025 revenue number by more than 6%, and with a lower expected growth rate over the next few years, my 2028 number is now almost 9% lower. I’m now looking for 3-5 year revenue growth in the 5% range and longer-term growth of a little more than 5%.

On the margin side, my expectations for FY’26 EBITDA margin have gone from around 23.5% to about 21% and a mid-20s percent EBITDA margin may not be possible within the next five years (though I would rather not underestimate potential leverage as the company exits this restructuring process and as products like BioBrace enter the mix). Likewise, my operating margin expectation for FY’25 is about 250bp lower now, and I’m no longer expecting double-digit ROCE within the next three years. All of this could get even worse if Conmed has to absorb the full impact of the threatened 25% tariff on goods imported from Mexico.

The good news, if you can call it that, is that the valuation was so undemanding before that there’s still a value argument to be made for the stock. Between discounted cash flow and margin/growth-driven EV/revenue, I can argue for a fair value of $79 to $81 against a current price in the low-$60s. I should note too that I’m using a lower EV/revenue multiple than what my multiple would normally support (2x versus 3x), as Conmed now fits into that slower-growing, weak margin leverage bucket where smaller stocks often languish with lower multiples.

The Bottom Line

Even with the full worst-case impact of tariffs, Conmed shares would still be modestly undervalued on discounted cash flow, so I can understand value-driven investors thinking they’ll just hunker down and ride out this rough patch.

I don’t necessarily disagree with that, but I’ve seen too many examples of slower-growing smaller med-techs turning into frustrating value traps to recommend that. I do still see multiple positive drivers here (in addition to valuation), but I think it may be best to take a step back for now, see what happens with tariffs, and see if management can drive some upside to revenue growth before getting more positive again.

data.title: BCD: A Longer Dated Strategy That Outperforms On The Commodities Market
data.description:
I generally avoid middle-ground solutions, especially with ETFs. However, I have to admit I'm increasingly appreciating a solution like Abrdn Bloomberg All Commodity Longer Dated Strategy K-1 Free ETF (NYSEARCA:BCD), even though it represents a soft compromise between short-term monetary funds and the commodities markets. In the last month, I've already expressed my interest in this approach while analyzing an alternative to BCD: PDBC. I must say that the current economic situation and its shifting prospects have once again directed my focus toward these types of funds, especially those that utilize a longer-dated strategy.

Longer-Dated Strategy vs. Standard Rolling Strategy ETF: An Important Insight to Keep in Mind

I was looking for an ETF in the commodities market with specific characteristics:

  • K-1 free.
  • Longer-dated strategy.

I found the BCD ETF interesting, although it is still not widely used: it has an AUM of only $252.92M.

However, it meets my research criteria. It is a K-1 free ETF with a longer-dated strategy, an expense ratio of 0.30%, a yield of 3.37%, and a 5Y yield growth rate of 24.28%.

I know that longer-dated strategy ETFs are less popular among commodity investors, but let me explain my choice: typically, this strategy is more effective in handling contango, which helps maintain lower volatility. And I know that this means a higher expense ratio and (typically) lower performance, but BCD appears to offer a reasonable trade-off.

It has maintained low volatility (9.56) and delivered strong performance compared to standard rolling ETFs. When comparing BCD to its standard rolling counterpart, BCI, the performance difference is striking—almost 30%. This is despite the higher cost and the (theoretically) lower ability to capture short-term market spikes in commodities.

Stagflation risk: manage it with BCD ETF

In recent days, a new concern has been developing in the stock market: this fear is called stagflation. I know, it's being mentioned in many analyses, and it sounds a bit excessive to me as well. However, the markets appear to be adopting this scenario - here's an interesting piece of evidence:

A decisive shift in trend compared to only a month ago

There are three notable developments in the market today:

  • A sudden shift in Treasury yields (-).
  • A surge in gold (commodities) prices (+).
  • A gradual depreciation of the US dollar (-).

Only one month ago:

  • Yields were rising (+).
  • Gold (commodities) was appreciating (+).
  • The dollar was reaching its highest levels of the year (+).
Markets End 2024 2025
Treasury - +
Commodities + +
Dollar + -

What's different now?

The market perspective has changed:

The economic data from January painted a picture of strong resilience in the US market. The inflation rate increased by 0.5%, reaching 3.1% YoY, while the unemployment rate has decreased to 4%. The writing was on the wall: the US economy was aligning with GDP growth expectations (over 2.1%), and the Fed was not going to cut rates.

However, the market opinion has changed again. This is probably driven by the drop in consumer confidence reported by the Conference Board, a strong dissonance compared to expectations.

This led to a decline in yields and a depreciation of the dollar, almost pricing in an incoming recession. It's no coincidence that recession expectations for the next year have risen to a nine-month high.

So, what's next?

I believe the consumer confidence data should not be underestimated. That said, as usual, I prefer to base my analysis on the data. The fact that commodity prices have maintained their trend while Treasuries and DXY have shifted is a strong bullish indicator. This suggests that (following the market movement) despite concerns about an economic slowdown, inflation expectations remain bullish. Put differently, I think that rather than a recession risk, a "stagflation" risk has come into play. This is bringing a lot of uncertainty to the markets, and "uncertainty" is typically an investor's greatest fear.

Personally, I looked for a solution in the only market that has shown strength (+) in both scenarios priced in by the market: commodities.

But why, specifically, could BCD serve as a balanced solution between these two scenarios?

Why BCD?

BCD invests in fixed income and commodities, tracking the performance of the Bloomberg Commodity Index 3-Month Forward Total Return.

I tried to reclassify the exposures of its benchmark to achieve this redistribution:

The outcome is a holding breakdown that I consider especially intriguing in light of the historical context ahead.

Indeed, this allocation creates a compelling balance: combining a yield of 3% with the commodities sector, historically strong during periods of high inflation, while mitigating potential declines that Treasuries might experience in a stagflation scenario.

Risk

As always, there is a risk to consider. The BCD ETF is illiquid, with an AUM of only $252.92M. This can lead to:

  1. Deviation from the NAV: compared to other ETFs, such as the well-known PDBC, it could face issues related to index tracking.
  2. A higher bid-ask spread than other ETFs: this could affect both purchase and sale transactions.

Therefore, when evaluating the cost associated with this ETF, it is prudent to also consider this aspect.

Conclusion

BCD ETF could potentially capture the benefits of high interest rates, take advantage of positive inflation, and possibly hedge against stagflation risks.

This is due to its well-balanced exposure and longer-dated strategy, which appears to maintain a controlled level of volatility without sacrificing returns.

data.title: Salesforce Q4 Performance Suggests Investors Should Look Elsewhere
data.description:
Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE:CRM) is a good company and fits the standards of companies that I tend to like a lot: wide moats, solid fundamentals, very reliable growth, and characteristics that allow the compounder status.

However, Q4 earnings were poor, revealing a fragility in its valuation, especially when compared to other companies with similar profiles.

Even considering the ~6% drop in the stock, Salesforce is not an appealing alternative for long-term compounding at this current price and deserves an additional discount, although it should still achieve consistent gains in the coming years.

Salesforce Q4 Earnings Were Poor

The Q4 earnings themselves weren't so bad as to justify the 6% drop in the stock on their own. Salesforce reported a revenue of $9.99B, missing consensus estimates by around $50 million. This revenue represents YOY growth of 8%, while non-GAAP revenue showed a change of 9%.

Its main revenue sources maintained strong momentum, with Sales and Service reporting 9% YOY growth, Platform and Other delivered 12%, while some segments such as Integration and Analytics (which includes MuleSoft and Tableau) showed a 6% change, with a weak point was Tableau, which advanced 3%.

In addition, the operating margin advanced by 0.7 percentage points to 18.2% (GAAP), due to gains in scale and consequent dilution of expenses, such as sales and marketing, which fell to 30% of revenue compared to 32% in the same period last year.

This margin advance allowed the company to deliver non-GAAP EPS of $2.78, beating market estimates by a good amount — ~$0.17, or 6.5%.

The real problem was the guidance for the next quarter. For Q1, Salesforce expects YOY growth to be between 6% and 7%. Although this is not so different from the full-year guidance, whose outlook is 7% and 8% YOY, going from ~12% and 10% growth as past quarters have shown to something between 6% and 7% is bad and affects the long term.

For example, market estimates suggest an average growth of 9.2% until the fiscal year 2027. Therefore, the guidance impacts the conviction that Salesforce will really be able to maintain top-line growth close to the double-digits for the next 3 years, not to mention the next 10 years.

Why I Think Salesforce Will Produce Steady Returns

Despite the uncertainty about the magnitude of top-line growth, there is still a strong conviction that this growth will be steady.

As is well known, Salesforce is very competent at what it does and has considerable customers all over the world. Most of the revenue generated is recurring (in Q4, ~95% of revenue was from subscription & support). These subscriptions are essential for the overwhelming majority of customers, whether it's sales services doing lead management and the like, marketing with marketing automation and campaign management or new features such as AI.

With so much data already on the platform and the system already implemented, there is a very high switching cost. It's very inefficient (and costly) for a customer to switch from Salesforce to another company, and this stickiness allows price increases with minimal churn.

With this already consolidated presence among large customers and expertise in different areas, additional solutions are also quite possible, i.e., cross-sell and up-sell can occur with greater frequency, also indicating prospects for stable growth in the coming years. An example of new services is Data Cloud & AI, whose annual recurring revenue reached $900 million in the fiscal year and delivered 120% YOY growth.

This makes the increased adoption of this type of software practically a secular trend, with projected CAGRs close to double-digits for this market over the coming years.

Even though this Q4 was only reasonable, a line from the CEO was added to the release, which highlights optimism about Salesforce's position in this market:

“No company is better positioned than Salesforce to lead customers through the digital labor revolution. With our deeply unified platform, seamlessly integrating our Customer 360 apps, Data Cloud and Agentforce, we’re already delivering unprecedented levels of productivity, efficiency and cost savings for thousands of companies.”

Although I think this sentence is somewhat optimistic, it still shows some of the company's values. Increasing efficiency and generating cost savings for big companies is critical and allows them to continue increasing their contracts with Salesforce, especially in these new features already mentioned.

These factors, together with the maturing of some segments, efficiency gains, and share repurchases, should lead to EPS rising slightly above revenue, increasing shareholder value.

Salesforce Valuation Isn’t Compelling Yet

A company that grows at a reasonable pace should be traded at a reasonable multiple, such as 20x. For companies that have sustainable double-digit growth, it is reasonable to pay a premium for this, such as 27x earnings forward or even 30x in more reliable cases like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT).

As Salesforce was growing more aggressively for a while, it was reasonable to pay this premium valuation, but as prospects have slowed down, this premium should be lower. I still think it should be because it's a company with wide moats and reliable prospects.

Salesforce's forward P/E is around ~26x, but with one important detail: it's non-GAAP. To get a sense of the magnitude, GAAP EPS in fiscal year 2025 was $6.36, but after adjustments that incorporate $3.27 of SBC and other factors, it rises to over $10 EPS. This price-to-earnings ratio allows an almost 4% earnings yield.

Assuming that sales growth remains between 8% and 7% until 2031 and the net income margin gradually expands, it's possible to find a P/E of 20.5x by the fiscal year 2029.

As growth is quite reliable, the company becomes a compounder, but this effect is not so apparent in a few years as the projected EPS growth in the table is below double-digit. Holding for almost a decade, the investor should be able to collect ~7% earnings yield, but remember, this is considering amortization and SBC as non-expenses.

In the long term, the effect is very apparent, with shares outstanding consistently increasing, but it's worth giving the benefit of the doubt since recent buybacks have controlled this.

The Bottom Line

I don't believe that CRM's valuation is completely unattractive. As I mentioned, the company should still be able to generate consistent returns for its shareholders since its fundamentals are still solid, there is predictable cash generation, and buybacks are being carried out.

However, when we consider that this growth should be below double-digit, it's difficult to be optimistic about receiving only ~4% over the coming years, and that's non-GAAP.

Another important factor is the opportunity cost. As much as it is a wide-moat company, there are others on the market with similar profiles that can offer a more promising future.

Microsoft has more aggressive growth prospects, greater revenue diversification (and consequently more optionalities), and this is for an only slightly higher forward P/E.

Intuit Inc. (INTU), too. Although its focus is different, it's also a high-recurrence software company with good margins, but unlike Salesforce, it's expected to grow above double-digit, and this for a multiple that's also only slightly higher.

In any case, the giant Salesforce is far from a bad company, and even with its questionable valuation, the company deserves a hold rating for its resilience.

data.title: TD's Chief Economist On Tariffs, Trade Relations And The Economic Implications
data.description:
It’s still the early days of the new U.S. administration, but we’ve already had a flurry of policy initiatives, including executive orders and numerous tariff announcements. Beata Caranci, Chief Economist with TD, speaks with MoneyTalk’s Kim Parlee about what this could mean for economic growth, monetary policy, and how the global outlook has changed.

Transcript

Kim Parlee - It's still the early days of the new US administration, but we've already had a flurry of policy initiatives, including executive orders and numerous tariff announcements, and that's been raising questions about the outlook for markets and the economy. TD Economics has come out with its latest report, and it's called "Questions-- We Have Answers, Addressing the Issues Impacting the Economic and Financial Outlook." And boy, oh, boy, do I have questions.

Joining us now is Beata Caranci, Chief Economist at TD, to run through this. Thanks for putting this out, first off, and thank you for coming in.

Beata Caranci - Thank you for having me.

Kim Parlee - OK, you have 10 questions here, and we're going to spend the next 22-odd minutes going through all of these in some detail. And I'm going to say, I'm going to be tilting a bit towards Canada as you go through this. But let's start with just the really big picture, and then we'll get into some great detail on Canada. You've got some really good information here. How has, for question one, the global economic outlook changed in light of the recent tariff developments? And I have to say, also, too, when I say recent, I mean recent is moment by moment.

Beata Caranci - Yeah. So I think everything that we're seeing on the global economic landscape with tariff escalation is leading to a markdown in the forecast. The one good aspect is that many economies, Canada included, were ending last year on a better note than the prior year. So there was a little bit of a tailwind coming into various economies in Europe and elsewhere.

So we're marking down the economic growth from a better watermark than would have been the case had this been a year ago. But ultimately, we took about 20 basis points off the forecast. You know, we had to make assumptions--

Kim Parlee - So when we say that-- this is the global forecast.

Beata Caranci - The global forecast, and it's worse for Canada. We had to make assumptions that-- we don't know particulars yet, but we did assume in our forecast that Europe was going to come into scope on tariffs in a much more active way than we've heard so far. China would remain in scope. They have 10% tariffs. That's likely to rise over time. And obviously, Canada and Mexico are already in scope.

So we didn't go full board on the assumptions with the full 25% tariffs on China-- I mean, sorry, on Canada and Mexico, on all products. We assumed, with time iteration on negotiations, that the Trump administration would likely move towards more product and industry-specific and country-specific initiatives in order to mitigate the cost to the US in terms of inflationary pressures. So we had to embed that assumption. We hope we're not wrong. Otherwise, the forecast would be more detrimentally impacted.

Kim Parlee - And I guess, when I look through the assumptions, which you just told us a little bit about them, too, what was interesting is the tariffs are assumed to remain in play for the remainder of this year before being largely phased out by mid 2026.

Beata Caranci - That's the hope.

Kim Parlee - But what I would say is, your job is simply always making assumptions and going through different scenarios. Just why that-- because I know you also say in here you're expecting some retaliation. You're also expecting governments, of course, to lean in and spend to try and prop things up. But why-- is the 2026 just because you hope that at some point-- yeah.

Beata Caranci - Yeah, well, there's a few things happening in the US. They have their own midterm elections coming in at the end of next year. And so it's going to be important for them to not transmit price impacts to consumers. That's where we've seen in history the population is less forgiving. And so doing things like taking pressure off your prices, which is actually an executive order under President Trump, to make the cost of living more affordable, seems to be that there is a little bit of a-- we guess there's a bit of a clock running down on how much time they could run through these tariffs.

Also, we're hoping that negotiations take place and that deals are cut between now and then. And when we look at the last Trump administration, it was about a year from when you saw tariffs come into place in the case of, say, Canada, in particular, and the USMCA negotiations to when that got finalized. So we're running through about a year-long process where we're allowing the negotiations and the price effects to play through and then hopefully some grounding back to normality.

Kim Parlee - Another piece you have in here-- I feel weird reading back your own report back to you. But you do hear of talk about this being on a time-lapse basis when you're looking at the tariffs. And you say that if full tariffs will remain in effect for six months, one year, and two years, you chose six years as the initial starting point. But you go on to say that you could see the Canadian economy tip into a recession, should the tariffs last too long, and unemployment rate peaking at 7.7% by the end of the year.

Beata Caranci - So that scenario analysis that we did-- so what I described previously was our base case forecast. And then we said, what if we're wrong here because there's so many moving parts? And this is a different administration than Trump 1.0.

Kim Parlee - Yep.

Beata Caranci - And so we thought, what if the 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico across all industries hold for six months? What does economic growth look like, and then at a year, and at two years? And the economy in Canada does tip into recession around the five to six month mark, if you're holding at that breadth and scale, not if you're holding at a lower level, like we said, where they start to get industry-specific, like the steel and aluminum tariffs that were announced.

So that would be almost now you're moving into your worst-case scenario of having longevity and breadth on tariffs. And so that's what we did in that section, just to give people a glimpse of what could transpire. At the same time, inflation metrics move up quite substantially. The US ends up worse off on the inflation front because they're starting from a higher point on inflation already. And so it's our hope that when you look at how administrations are looking at this--

Kim Parlee - They will feel the pain on that side.

Beata Caranci - Yeah, who wants to impart 4% inflation over to their population, when literally, in the US, they're really price sensitive, complaining right now about the price of a carton of eggs, which is $7 in the US in places like New York. So imagine if that spreads broadly to all products.

Kim Parlee - I want to talk just quickly before we go to break about central banker reaction. Sorry. The question is-- I have to do verbatim-- how would central bankers react to trade war scenarios? And I'm going to maybe focus more on the Bank of Canada. What would you expect?

Beata Caranci - Yeah, so the Bank of Canada has put out quite a bit of information on this, basically, non-committal. They're not saying they would cut rates or raise or leave because they're saying, we have to see how the transmission flows through the duality of higher prices versus weaker economic growth.

We're betting that they would take a risk management approach to this, saying that it's more likely you're going to move towards deep economic contraction as time goes on. And that overplays any temporary price effect from that one-time level shift in tariffs feeding through to consumer prices.

Kim Parlee - Having said that, we know from the pandemic that people internalize prices very differently than how an economist would think about it. Oh, it's a one-time level price. But at the end of the day, someone's saying, well, I'm paying $8 a carton for milk. So they're not happy about it.

Beata Caranci - Because they're not buying anything else.

Kim Parlee - Exactly. And then those inflation expectations become entrenched. They stay higher for longer, even as prices come down. And that's the effect we saw in the pandemic, where you saw wage negotiations, that process continue on for two to three years once you're even past that peak point on inflation.

Beata Caranci - So that is the concern the Bank of Canada would have. So we don't expect that, even if we move towards a recession, I think people think that they would be cutting rates aggressively. We don't think they would. They would still remain cautious.

Our base case is they can get to about two and a quarter on the policy rate. In the event that we move towards a recession, we might see something about 1.5% on the policy rate, but nothing like we saw in the past where they moved towards zero because they would want to really-- interest rates are a single tool. They would be counting on the government to do more of the heavy lifting.

Kim Parlee - Next question, Beata, with regards to what's happening with the US administration and Canada. How have markets reacted so far to tariff threats? And I want to focus here, if we could, more on currencies and the loonie. What have you seen? What can we get to?

Beata Caranci - Yeah so what we saw when that blanket 25% tariff was going to come in into effect-- it was February 1, 2, 3, 4th, I think it was and announced over the weekend on a Saturday-- on that Monday, one day ahead of it going live, we saw the currency slip down to about $0.67. Now, that trading was reversed because by 10:00 AM, Mexico's president had already received word that their side of the tariffs were going to be delayed, which led everybody to believe the same was eventually going to happen on the Canadian side.

I think if you had let the market run through with ongoing tariffs and we didn't get that delayed 30 days, it would not have surprised me to see a $0.65 loonie. And then some people are arguing possibly more to get down to offset that 25% price premium now that's occurring on the tariffs. So it's not a good news story. The loonie would have to weaken. It is a natural dynamic, so you could regain some competitiveness through that side of the price transfer side between the countries.

As it stands now, because the threat is not yet quite live, we saw the loonie immediately bounce back, back toward $0.70, which tells you where markets feel comfortable. They're still discounting it for uncertainty. Otherwise, it would have been probably somewhere between $0.72 and $0.75, so we still think there's a discount being placed. But it gives you a really good sense of how markets are thinking. If we go live with the 25% tariffs, we're going to be right back at a minimum to $0.67.

Kim Parlee - Yeah, OK. Let's move on to the-- I think this is one of the most important questions you have in here. We're going to skip question four, five and six. It's a great report. You can find a copy and get those ones yourself. We're going number seven. How is Canada's economy performing in the face of elevated uncertainty? And I think this is the biggie.

Beata Caranci - Yeah, this is interesting because this goes back to the point that, had we not had this risk, we would have done a substantial upgrade to the Canadian outlook because the second half of last year ended quite strongly with consumer spending well over 3%. So consumers are back in the mood of spending. Interest rates were coming down. The housing market was rebounding, really, responsiveness to what was happening in the economy. The job market was recovering, doing much better. Unemployment rate was starting to fall. It's down about 30 basis points from its peak.

And then January came along, and we saw the housing market stumble in February. And so we're starting to get a sense that there is a confidence channel being impacted here that, even though people maintain their jobs and the risk is not yet filtering directly--

Kim Parlee - But on the investment side.

Beata Caranci - --the decisions are being delayed. And even some surveys are out there demonstrating that about 70% of companies that were surveyed are saying they're hitting the pause button on investment right now to get a sense of which way this is going to go. And a number of companies are assessing that if tariffs were to come in at 25%, do they need to move operations in about 50%, said they would have to think about that movement of investment over to the US.

So these are quite large numbers that we're talking about, which is unfortunate because the economy itself was probably going to have a very good year at over 2% growth. And we're going to have some kind of wine handle on it now.

Kim Parlee - Yeah, at least it started off in a stronger place to start with. So I'm just going to start with that. I want to bring up this chart because, again, as someone who grew up in New Brunswick, I did a double take on this one. But your team put this together. And the question is, how are different provinces exposed to tariff risks? This is question eight. And there's the question, and we'll bring up the chart. Alberta and New Brunswick, one third of exports. Sorry. One third of their nominal GDP are exports to the US.

Beata Caranci - Yeah, to US exports. So Alberta's resources, obviously.

Kim Parlee - Obvious, yeah.

Beata Caranci - And there's an element of that in New Brunswick, as well. And you also see Saskatchewan on there. So anything that's resource oriented is going to have more appeal on the export market to the US and have more of its economy tilted to that. Then, of course, Ontario makes the list off of the manufacturing side and the auto sector in particular.

Kim Parlee - Well, let's bring that one up because actually, I think we have the sort of the job picture, same conversation, different slice. They can look at that. Yeah. You can see just the number of jobs tied to the auto sector. Again, this is not new news to anybody, I'm sure, who's been paying attention, but it certainly quantifies it a bit.

Beata Caranci - Yeah, it quantifies it. And it also tells you that it would be very difficult for governments to backstop all of this, meaning that if we are going to go through an industrial rotation, as Canadian companies oriented to the US shift to the US and vice versa, new companies appear in Canada that service the Canadian market that are being displaced from the US side, these numbers are too large for federal and provincial governments to backstop wages on a medium- or long-term basis, meaning, when we were talking earlier about what happens if this goes on for six months or a year, just with if our exports were to drop by a third to the US and you look at the job displacements, that could be over 600,000 jobs.

That would cost us about $2.5 billion a day just in wage subsidies to-- sorry, a month-- to support that. So there's not unlimited resources here. And so we would have to think through more creatively what we envision for the country as we go forward because these are fairly large numbers.

Kim Parlee - And just on that last point in the report, you point out the amount that we're selling to the States. But then you actually stay down here-- and I think it's material that they talk about imports from the US equate to 23% and 11% of GDP in Manitoba and Saskatchewan, meaning that if they don't have the export impact, they're also going to be impacted by the imports that they can't get anymore. Or they have to pay more for it to get at the same time.

Beata Caranci - And, well, there's where the cheap dollar starts to come in all of a sudden because you're bringing in-- you're importing the inflation dynamic to make it harder. And then if you're adding on top of that retaliatory tariffs, you're bringing in the additional prices there. So you can see why this suddenly becomes quite inflationary and not palatable to sustain on a long-term basis, which is also what we're banking on in the US, that the US doesn't have the same export exposure as Canada.

But the majority of states do see Canada as their primary export market. So it is material at the state level. And that's why you hear a bit more conversation by governors at states saying, we've got to be a little bit more thoughtful about this.

Kim Parlee - How could the government pivot policy to provide insulation to Canada? And you just finished telling me that they can't backstop everything. So what do you see?

Beata Caranci - Well, they have to break it out into, what is the short-term supports you can provide to businesses and households? And by short term, you can be talking a matter of months. And then what is your long-term vision for where you need to take the country as a whole in terms of competitiveness and this rotation that's going to be required?

On short-term issues, if you look at what was done in the past, the pandemic gives you a little bit of reference of policies that are ready to go. But they do have to be legislated, like things like wage subsidies. And we don't currently have a federal government sitting. So you can't just pull that one out of the gate.

But we have EI. And you could do, without any new legislation, job sharing tactics, where you increase the amount of hours that are permissible under job sharing, which was done during the steel and aluminum tariffs under Trump 1.0, where they went from you can job share from 36 hours to 76 hours. So you can try to prevent layoffs and give companies a bit more flexibility in how they're using their existing labor force. So those are things right out of the gate, not going to cost you any more money. It's already in there.

And then you would have to think through, are you going to do wage subsidies? Or is it actually better at this stage to think through how to incent businesses to invest and keep the money in the country? One thing when you're thinking through supporting through incomes, people may choose to take that income and buy products that you're actually buying from the US. And now you've literally just subsidized a purchase over to the US.

Kim Parlee - Interesting, yeah.

Beata Caranci - And so you've lost the domestic multiplier inside the country. So one of the recommendations we hear more and more about is thinking through what does that domestic multiplier look like. With every dollar coming out of government coffers, does it stay in the country? Could you put in investment incentives for all businesses?

Because you have one segment of the economy suffering who may not take advantage of it at all. But you have a large portion that could and provide an offset and help you get those engines going, especially knowing that a lot of companies are delaying investment right now. And can you incent some of that?

And so that's your near term. You'll be doing tax holidays, GST holidays for inputs and other factors. And then the vision. And this is where people get into making sure we have no interprovincial barriers that are inhibiting investment and growth in Canada, ensuring our productivity is at the maximum it could be, so looking at income and corporate tax structures.

And a big one that is really not going to come in immediate but over stages is thinking about delayering of regulation. And there have been studies that show that if you look at regulation growth, the layering, every year, you have more and more regulation come up on top of industry.

Kim Parlee - It's suffocating.

Beata Caranci - It is, and it grows at about 2% a year. And it's estimated by stat scanning, I think KPMG, that it has reduced investment by 10% lower than it otherwise would be. And it also reduces business entry, new business formation or business dynamism. So there's a lot of work to be done there. But as you know, that's not going to happen on day 2 or day 50. That's something that you have to be really committed to continuing on over the course of years to delayering that regulation.

So that's the vision portion of what governments can do. And it's probably the most important area because people are going to end up suffering no matter what. But you hope that in doing so, they have something positive to come out to from an economy that's more dynamic and showing faster growth.

Kim Parlee - It's fascinating. I've only got about a minute and a half here, Beata, so I want to ask you about Canadian housing. So the last one is, what range of outcomes can we reasonably expect for the Canadian housing market this year?

Beata Caranci - Yeah. So we were optimistic up until December 30, January 20, I guess. And like I said, that data for end of December and into January definitely show that there's a sentiment factor coming through. We don't know 100% if it was just a lull. But it looks like that, in January in particular, home sales were actually doing OK until that final week of January, when that 25% tariff threat became quite real to people.

And then in February, unfortunately, we had some bad weather in Ontario, Quebec and across the east coast. So there's going to be weak sales in there. So we're going to get a little bit of confusion on the signals. But overall, we originally thought that sales could grow double digit this year from the lower interest rates.

But now we've cut that back. We had 16% growth in sales, and we're thinking maybe we get 5% growth in sales. This is a big revision, and that's largely because of that weak hand-off that's coming in off of those December and January sales. And the same for prices.

We thought that price growth could be somewhere around 5% to 7%. And that looks less likely if your sales are going to weaken. So we might have more of a flat market to maybe 3% growth. So it's going to require those entering the market to have steely nerves to get in and understand that--

Kim Parlee - And understand their time frames.

Beata Caranci - That's right.

Kim Parlee - The people who are buying.

Beata Caranci - That's right. And have a sense of job security because it'll be much more difficult for people on the trade side.

Original Post

data.title: The Mosaic Company 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by The Mosaic Company in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: INDY: Weathering The Storm With Indian Mega Caps
data.description:
There have been a lot of concerns about Indian growth in recent months. To some extent, warranted, given the spiking inflation and below-par GDP prints toward the end of 2024. But even in a downcycle, it’s worth keeping in mind that India is still on track to be the fastest-growing large economy (projected 6.3-6.8% ‘real’ GDP growth or >10% nominal). Plus, with leading indicators, from rapidly decelerating food prices, interest rate cuts, and tax reliefs from this year’s union budget, turning supportive, most signs point to a domestic upturn from here.

The key economic threat is really external, from a new round of tariffs by President Trump to a strengthening US Dollar. To be clear, India remains a relative beneficiary from external turbulence, as evidenced by its capitalizing on supply chain shifts away from China and accessing cheap Russian energy in recent years. And while the US is a key Indian export market, exposure (just over 2% of GDP) is a lot smaller than other emerging markets, so any direct impact is limited.

For now, the market has mostly looked through a lot of these fundamental positives, de-rating Indian large-caps back below their historical averages. For a group of stocks with a track record of compounding earnings in the mid-teens, while also sustaining high teen returns on (unlevered) equity through the cycles, this seems a tad unwarranted.

Having, relatively speaking, outperformed the other Indian ETFs since I last covered the fund (see INDY: India's Lagging Uber Caps Are Worth A Look), iShares India 50 ETF (NASDAQ:INDY) continues to stand out as a more defensive, higher-quality vehicle to ride out the storm.

INDY Overview – Tracking India's Flagship Domestic Index

There are quite a few vehicles for passive Indian large-cap exposure, from the largest and most liquid option, iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA), to Franklin Templeton's ultra-low-cost Franklin FTSE India ETF (FLIN). Neither are quite as concentrated on India’s mega caps as INDY, though, which tracks India's flagship capitalization-weighted Nifty 50 index.

The rest of INDY’s fundamentals don’t quite measure up, from its premium expense ratio to its thinner liquidity, so investing in this ETF really comes down to the kind of Indian large-cap exposure investors want.

Assets

Expense Ratio

Bid/Ask Spread (30-Day Median)

iShares India 50 ETF

$831m

0.89%

0.08%

iShares MSCI India ETF

$8,341m

0.62%

0.02%

Franklin Templeton FTSE India ETF

1,620m

0.19%

0.03%

Source: iShares, Franklin Templeton

INDY Portfolio – Puts and Takes Ahead of the March Rebalance

As INDY only tracks fifty stocks, the fund tends to run much higher concentration levels at both the sector and single-stock levels. As things stand, the Financial sector remains the key sector with an overweighting at 35.6%, followed by Information Technology (up to 13.4%) and Energy (down to 10.2%). Elsewhere, there’s quite a bit of consumer exposure as well - Discretionary (up to 9.9%) and Staples (7.7%) combine for a 17.5% allocation.

Now, INDY's outsized Financial overweight will likely increase after the Nifty 50’s next rebalancing in March, which will see food products company Britannia and state-owned Bharat Petroleum Corporation removed in favor of Jio Financial Services and food delivery platform Zomato. Also, broader conditions are improving for Indian banks under the new RBI regime, so investors will want to be comfortable with an even higher Financial allocation.

Nifty 50 Rebalancing Inclusion

Zomato

JioFin

Nifty 50 Rebalancing Exclusion

BPCL

Britannia

Source: Nuvama

At the single-stock level, INDY’s leading positions remain in India’s two leading private banks - HDFC Bank Limited (HDB) and ICICI Bank Limited (IBN) at 12.6% and 8.5%, respectively. Diversified conglomerate Reliance Industries (RLNIY) has lost ground at 8.0% after a poor year, while IT services leader Infosys Limited (INFY) is up to 6.3% on hopes of a tech spending recovery (see Infosys: Quality Large-Cap Levered To An IT Spending Recovery).

While the top five concentration is relatively higher than other India ETFs at 39.7%, INDY’s largest holdings tend to stay constant; thus, investors looking for sustained and very concentrated exposure to India's highest-quality franchises will find a lot to like here.

INDY Performance – Defensive Qualities Shine Through

In India, growth comes from the smaller caps, so in bull markets, mega-cap-focused INDY has lagged. Still, this is a fund that has compounded at an annualized +8.2% and +5.9% over the last five and ten years, respectively. And during bear markets, like we’ve seen in recent months, INDY has proven to be a lot more resilient. Year-to-date, INDY has ‘only’ declined by -5.1% (vs. -7.1% for INDA and -8.0% for FLIN), while over the last six months, the fund’s -8.3% decline is again the pick of the large-cap ETFs (-10.0% for INDA and -9.8% for FLIN).

Another source of downside protection for INDY is its tracking error vs. the Nifty 50 benchmark. On the one hand, this performance delta, mainly the result of capital gains taxes, tends to eat into returns in bull markets. But capital taxes work both ways, so this delta reverses in bear markets, allowing INDY to outperform its benchmark this year. Adding to INDY’s defensiveness is its larger banking overweight, which keeps the fund’s beta low through the cycles.

On a relative basis, INDY also tends to trade at a lower forward P/E than other India trackers. As for historical comparisons, this year’s correction has brought Nifty 50 multiples back down to the high teens (in line with long-term averages) – arguably cheap for a group of stocks on track for mid-teens earnings growth and with a demonstrated high-teens ROE track record.

Summing Up

India’s domestic fundamentals are turning, helped by policy support and a rapidly decelerating inflation situation. External headwinds are another matter entirely and equities won’t be immune from the fallout of a trade/tariff war near-term. While not the fastest horse, INDY’s focus on the highest quality Indian mega caps offers a safer way to ride things out in the meantime.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Follow The Institutional Money And Capex Of Big Tech
data.description:
Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) has had a wild ride so far in 2025. Investor confidence improved after an independent special committee found no management wrongdoing, and Nasdaq granted Super Micro Computer an extension to file its annual report by February 25, 2025. One large indicator to follow was institutional ownership. Firstly, institutional ownership was chopped in half when EY resigned as auditor. However, when the final deadline closed in and more reassurance was given by management, institutional ownership rose enormously. As a result, the stock price doubled at the beginning of the year from $30 to a high of $60. Later, Super Micro Computer's stock price came back down after delivering the missing filings, which made the stock an attractive investment. Investors should consider following institutional money.

The verdict

Super Micro Computer filed the missing annual report and latest quarterly reports before the extension deadline given by Nasdaq. BDO USA mentions in their audit opinion the following:

We have audited the accompanying consolidated balance sheet of Super Micro Computer, Inc. (the “Company”) as of June 30, 2024, the related consolidated statements of operations, comprehensive income, stockholders’ equity, and cash flows for the year then ended, and the related notes (collectively referred to as the “consolidated financial statements”). In our opinion, the consolidated financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of the Company at June 30, 2024, and the results of its operations and its cash flows for the year then ended, in conformity with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.

Super Micro Computer's stock price is still far away from the $120 price it had before the accounting issues, which leaves room for price appreciation in the coming days or weeks.

Data center Capex

Super Micro Computer's business has an immense tailwind due to increasing investments in data center capital expenditures. To name a few, Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), (GOOGL) is expected to spend $75 billion on capex in 2025, Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) will increase its capex to $100 billion, Microsoft (MSFT) to $80 billion specifically into AI-enabled data centers and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) up to $65 billion (all mostly for the AI data center build-out).

Charles Liang mentioned in the latest earnings report, Q2 2025, the following:

With our leading direct-liquid cooling (DLC) technology and over 30% of new data centers expected to adopt it in the next 12 months, Supermicro is well-positioned to grow AI infrastructure design wins based on NVIDIA Blackwell and more.

Furthermore, Charles Liang lays out the guidance of $23.5 to $25 billion revenue in FY2025 and $40 billion revenue in FY2026, which would respectively represent a 60% and 66% year-over-year increase compared to the FY 2024 revenue of $15 billion.

Valuation

Historically speaking SMCI is undervalued after the accounting debacle. The price-to-earnings multiple is trading around the company's historical average of 19.5x. At the same time, the company has increased its competitive advantages with advanced technology direct-liquid cooling (product differentiation). Direct-liquid cooling offers energy efficiency, cost savings and improved performance. This alone should demand a higher multiple. So far, the stock is still trading below the sector median.

AI has boosted the company's sales and this is not looking to slow down anytime soon with big tech companies pushing for more capex spending. The current year-over-year growth rate is way beyond the average of 28%. Yet, a revaluation of increased growth has not come to fruition.

More so, net income also benefited from the higher demand. The net income margin increased from the 5-year average of 5.5% to 8%. These margins are not yet Nvidia-like, but there is room for margin expansion if demand for the company's products increases in the coming months. The current ratio of 3.7x indicates that there is a good amount of working capital to grow revenues.

Risks

There are a couple of risk factors to consider. Competition is definitely present with players like Dell, IBM, and HP trying to take a piece of the data center market. These companies have more than enough resources to threaten Super Micro Computer. Super Micro Computer's strength will be their pure focus on data centers. Nonetheless, larger firms can bundle their hardware with software and cloud services, creating a better full-round service.

The current AI infrastructure spending growth is fueling SMCI's revenue. If companies shift to more efficient AI compute models, that could result in fewer need of servers. In that case, SMCI might underperform and fall short of guidance.

More accounting issues in the future will quickly erode investor confidence. This is probably the last chance the company gets to fully fix their internal accounting and governance issues. Investors should not take this lightly and follow it up closely.

Takeaway

Super Micro Computer is well-positioned to expand their business in the AI-driven data center boom. Large companies are willing to build out massive AI infrastructure and SMCI is at the forefront to benefit. The latest accounting debacle created a unique value proposition to build a position in one of the fastest-growing companies.

Therefore, I rate Super Micro Computer a "BUY" at $45 a share.

data.title: Amazon: In-House Chips Could Be The New Ace In The Hole
data.description:
The Amazon Investment Thesis

Amazon.com Inc (NASDAQ:AMZN), like many other big tech companies, seems to be busy trying to figure out how to reduce its dependence on Nvidia (NVDA). So it makes sense that quantum computing or in-house chips would be something to focus on. And I think that these two areas have the potential to be the next ace in the hole.

Right now, Nvidia has a lot of pricing power, and having its own chips would be very beneficial to Amazon in the long run, which could lead to multiple expansions as well as margin improvements.

AMZN & Quantum Computing

After Google (GOOG) recently unveiled its Willow quantum computing chip and Microsoft (MSFT) recently unveiled its Majorana 1 chip, it's now Amazon's turn to present their project. The lead designer is the AWS Center for Quantum Computing, which launched in 2019 and expanded with a new facility at the California Institute of Technology in 2021.

The prototype, which has now been unveiled by Amazon, is called Ocelot and promises to be more cost-effective. In addition, it appears that the current architecture would also allow for many more qubits to be placed on it, which would in turn advance the entire quantum ecosystem. Oscar Painter, director of hardware at AWS Quantum, had this to say:

In the future, quantum chips built according to the Ocelot architecture could cost as little as one-fifth of current approaches, due to the drastically reduced number of resources required for error correction. Concretely, we believe this will accelerate our timeline to a practical quantum computer by up to five years.

I find it particularly interesting that he assumes that this could shorten the timeline by 5 years. One of my points of criticism in my articles on quantum computing companies was that in my opinion the market readiness was very far in the future and it would still take years. IBM (IBM), which I consider to be a pioneer in the field of quantum computing, has published the following development roadmap.

At the time, IBM estimated that quantum computers with thousands of qubits would not be available until 2033, making them a long way off. Jensen Huang, the CEO of Nvidia, has also confirmed this indirectly by saying that he believes that usable quantum computers are still a decade away.

Leading experts in this field also assume a period of 10 to 20 years. However, if Oscar Painter's prediction of a 5-year reduction in development time is correct, this period would be reduced to 5 to 15 years. This means that, in the ideal case of further breakthroughs, quantum computers could be marketable for a broader mass of people before 2030. And I think more breakthroughs are not unlikely, because many companies would benefit greatly if Nvidia's monopoly on chips were broken. So I think there will be more big R&D spending in quantum computing. IBM, for example, has spent 7% of its total R&D budget on quantum computing in 2023.

Quantum chips, Nvidia and margins

Normally I agree with Jensen Huang when it comes to technical specifications. But in the area of quantum chips, I think Nvidia has a huge interest in maintaining the status quo and keeping the Magnificent 7 and other big companies dependent on how many high-performance chips they get from Nvidia. Nvidia has an incredible amount of market power and pricing power here, which of course erodes margins for customers.

Nvidia's profit margins have skyrocketed over the last five years, as the company has been able to price its products at virtually will. And with a profit margin of 55%, we can see what an incredible amount of savings there would be if companies could make powerful chips in-house. Whether it be quantum chips or AI chips. Companies like Amazon, Google or Meta (META) could save billions in CapEx, which would have a very positive impact on their own margins.

But I think it will be a while before there is a big threat to Nvidia, and I expect Nvidia to continue to be the dominant force for the next three years. But in the long term, I could see Nvidia's market position being challenged. Because the incentives are just incredibly high.

Amazon's EPS Estimates

Current EPS estimates suggest that growth will slow somewhat in 2030, and could be as low as 17% from 2029 to 2030. And that would be well below the average of the last 5 years, which is currently at a 36% CAGR for diluted EPS. If we now assume that by 2030 it could be possible for Amazon to manufacture competitive quantum or AI chips, I would strongly assume that this would have a positive impact on EPS.

This means that any additional positive news that Amazon releases in this regard increases the likelihood that the current analyst estimates are too conservative. And that would imply that the upside is higher than thought and the stock could be undervalued right now.

In addition, there is still the possibility to buy one of the promising companies in the field of quantum computing through M&A, if they have a breakthrough, or if the numerous venture investments in the field bear fruit. In fact, 2024 was the year with the most investments in quantum computing startups ever. More than $1.5 billion was invested, twice as much as in 2023.

Therefore, it is not out of the question that there could be more quantum computing IPOs in the future that aim to capitalize on the hype, as quantum computing could be about where AI was a few years ago. And the more positive news that comes out about the sector, the more attractive it would be for private companies to go public because they could benefit from higher prices due to the hype.

The explosive rise in the share prices of some quantum computing companies at the end of last year clearly shows that positive news attracts capital to this area. Some companies have increased in value tenfold or twentyfold in a very short time. Then, as you can see, a lot of capital was withdrawn because the stock prices fell again. Nevertheless, the above peer group significantly outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY) last year.

And I think that there will be more of these phases as we get closer to commercialization, because the news about quantum computers seems to be getting more and more frequent lately.

Valuation - Could in-house chips affect the multiple?

Amazon currently trades at its cheapest multiple over the last 5 years, well below the 5-year average of 84x. So I would argue that better margins from in-house chips would have a strong impact on the multiple.

In fact, AWS CapEx more than doubled from 2023 to 2024, from $24,843 to $53,267, and if Amazon could reduce that CapEx spending with in-house chips, that would be a huge savings, especially considering that AWS is still growing at a rapid pace. But also the other areas in the company would benefit from in-house Chips so the total savings potential would be gigantic.

I would even go so far as to say that I could see in-house chips combined with further growth in AWS driving Amazon's profit margin from the current 9.29% to 12% to 15%.

Conclusion

With Gravitron, Tranium, and Inferentia, Amazon is trying to shake up the market, and perhaps their chip manufacturing intentions will result in a new AWS-like situation for them. The demand for cheap powerful chips that are not tied to Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem is definitely there. While CUDA is currently more powerful than competing software offerings, open source solutions often win out in the long run.

Annapurna Labs, which has been part of Amazon since 2015, is certainly working on good solutions, and the odds are not bad that Amazon will be successful with them. And even if Amazon's chips were only used by Amazon for AWS and not sold to other companies, it would still be a huge success because the cost savings are huge.

Aside from this huge potential opportunity, Amazon remains the largest online retailer in the Western world, with a brand that generates consistent free cash flow that can be invested in growth.

However, it remains clear, and the Bloomberg interview with the AWS CEO confirms this, that Nvidia is still the non plus ultra at the moment, and that Amazon's chips should be seen primarily as a kind of support for customers who are looking for inexpensive products with good, but not top performance. But with Trainium 2 being up to 40% cheaper than Nvidia's GPUs and Trainium 3 likely to offer performance improvements, a competitor could slowly and stealthily emerge.
